examples.title: Queue Array Implementation - Basic Structure
examples.description: Complete queue implementation using static array with all basic operations
examples.code:
// Queue Implementation Using Arrays
// MAXSIZE = 5

DECLARE ARRAY queue[MAXSIZE]
DECLARE INTEGER front = -1
DECLARE INTEGER rear = -1

// Check if queue is empty
FUNCTION isEmpty()
 IF front == -1 OR front > rear THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
END FUNCTION

// Check if queue is full
FUNCTION isFull()
 IF rear == MAXSIZE - 1 THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
END FUNCTION

// Add element to queue (enqueue)
FUNCTION enqueue(item)
 IF isFull() THEN
 PRINT "Queue Overflow! Cannot enqueue " + item
 RETURN
 END IF
 
 IF front == -1 THEN
 front = 0 // First element
 END IF
 
 rear = rear + 1
 queue[rear] = item
 PRINT item + " enqueued successfully"
END FUNCTION

// Remove element from queue (dequeue)
FUNCTION dequeue()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 PRINT "Queue Underflow! Queue is empty"
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 
 item = queue[front]
 
 IF front == rear THEN
 // Last element - reset queue
 front = -1
 rear = -1
 ELSE
 front = front + 1
 END IF
 
 PRINT item + " dequeued successfully"
 RETURN item
END FUNCTION

// View front element without removing
FUNCTION peek()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 PRINT "Queue is empty"
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 RETURN queue[front]
END FUNCTION

// Display queue elements
FUNCTION display()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 PRINT "Queue is empty"
 RETURN
 END IF
 
 PRINT "Queue elements: "
 FOR i FROM front TO rear DO
 PRINT queue[i] + " <- "
 END FOR
 PRINT "(Front)"
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This code implements a queue using a static array. Key points: front and rear pointers track queue boundaries, isEmpty checks if front > rear or front == -1, isFull checks if rear == MAXSIZE-1, enqueue adds at rear index, dequeue removes from front index and resets pointers when queue becomes empty.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for all operations (enqueue, dequeue, isEmpty, isFull, peek)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n = MAXSIZE (array of fixed size)

examples.title: Queue Implementation with Size Tracking
examples.description: Alternative implementation that tracks current number of elements
examples.code:
// Queue Implementation with Size Counter
// More elegant overflow/empty detection

DECLARE ARRAY queue[MAXSIZE]
DECLARE INTEGER front = 0
DECLARE INTEGER rear = -1
DECLARE INTEGER size = 0

FUNCTION isEmpty()
 RETURN size == 0
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION isFull()
 RETURN size == MAXSIZE
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION getSize()
 RETURN size
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION enqueue(item)
 IF isFull() THEN
 PRINT "Queue Overflow!"
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
 
 rear = (rear + 1) % MAXSIZE // Circular advancement
 queue[rear] = item
 size = size + 1
 RETURN TRUE
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION dequeue()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 PRINT "Queue Underflow!"
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 
 item = queue[front]
 front = (front + 1) % MAXSIZE // Circular advancement
 size = size - 1
 RETURN item
END FUNCTION

// Note: This implementation uses circular advancement
// and can be easily converted to true circular queue
examples.explanation: This version uses a size counter which simplifies empty/full detection. The circular advancement (using modulo) is shown here as preparation for circular queue implementation. This approach elegantly solves the front == rear ambiguity problem.

examples.title: Queue Application - Customer Service System
examples.description: Practical application of queue using arrays for customer waiting management
examples.code:
// Queue Application: Restaurant Waiting System
// Simulates customer waiting for tables

CONSTANT MAX_CUSTOMERS = 20

STRUCTURE Customer
 STRING name
 INTEGER partySize
 INTEGER arrivalTime
END STRUCTURE

DECLARE ARRAY waitingQueue[MAX_CUSTOMERS]
DECLARE INTEGER front = -1
DECLARE INTEGER rear = -1

// Add customer to waiting list
FUNCTION addToWaitingList(name, partySize, currentTime)
 IF rear == MAX_CUSTOMERS - 1 THEN
 PRINT "Sorry, waiting list is full!"
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
 
 DECLARE customer
 customer.name = name
 customer.partySize = partySize
 customer.arrivalTime = currentTime
 
 IF front == -1 THEN
 front = 0
 END IF
 
 rear = rear + 1
 waitingQueue[rear] = customer
 PRINT name + " added to waiting list. Position: " + (rear - front + 1)
 RETURN TRUE
END FUNCTION

// Serve next customer (table becomes available)
FUNCTION serveNextCustomer()
 IF front == -1 OR front > rear THEN
 PRINT "No customers waiting!"
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 
 customer = waitingQueue[front]
 
 IF front == rear THEN
 front = -1
 rear = -1
 ELSE
 front = front + 1
 END IF
 
 PRINT "Serving: " + customer.name + " (Party of " + customer.partySize + ")"
 RETURN customer
END FUNCTION

// Display waiting list
FUNCTION showWaitingList()
 IF front == -1 OR front > rear THEN
 PRINT "No customers in waiting list"
 RETURN
 END IF
 
 PRINT "Current Waiting List:"
 FOR i FROM front TO rear DO
 PRINT i-front+1 + ". " + waitingQueue[i].name + 
 " (Party: " + waitingQueue[i].partySize + ")"
 END FOR
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This demonstrates a practical application - a restaurant waiting system using queue. It stores customer information including name, party size, and arrival time. The queue ensures fair service (FIFO) based on arrival order. Limitations include fixed size and inability to handle priority customers.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for add/serve operations, O(n) for displaying entire list
